My Story

30 YEARS IN SPORT. ONE LESSON LEARNED.

I am Ben Cullen. I have been involved in sport for 30 years — not just in one discipline.

I competed as a semi-professional footballer, represented my county as a high jumper, was pre-selected in 2009 for the London 2012 Olympics across handball, rowing and volleyball, competed as an indoor competitive rower, and spent 20 years on the World Tour hitting with Grand Slam doubles champions.

I currently coach at the UK's number one ranked tennis school and hold elite coaching qualifications across tennis, strength and conditioning and speed agility and quickness — a combination almost no coach in the UK holds.

The single most consistent pattern across every sport: the athletes who move better, react faster and have genuine athletic foundations consistently outperform those who simply practise their sport more.

Ben Cullen Athletics is the result of 30 years living that truth. Speed. Agility. Power. Coordination. Balance. These are universal. And they are trainable.

The Missing Piece

WHY SPEED & AGILITY
CHANGES EVERYTHING.

Most junior athletes never train the foundations that make every sport better. Here is why that matters.

The Problem

MORE SPORT IS NOT
THE ANSWER.

Parents invest thousands in sport-specific coaching. More tennis. More football. More matches. And yet their child still gets beaten by athletes who move differently. React faster. Get to balls they cannot reach.

The frustrating truth is that practising a sport more does not develop the underlying athletic qualities that make that sport possible at a higher level.

Speed. Agility. Reaction time. Explosive power. These are foundations every sport is built on — and they require dedicated, specific training to develop.

30+
Years of Pattern Recognition
Coaching across multiple sports at every level — the athletes who move better consistently outperform those who simply practise more.
No.1
Currently at the UK's Top Ranked Tennis School
Working with elite junior players daily confirms what the research shows — athletic foundations are the differentiator at every level.
10–14
The Golden Window for Speed Development
The nervous system is most receptive to speed and agility training between ages 10-14. Miss this window and development is significantly harder.
The Science

WHAT SPEED TRAINING
ACTUALLY DOES.

The science behind why dedicated athletic training produces lasting results

🧠
Neural Adaptation

Speed training rewires the nervous system to recruit muscle fibres faster. A neurological adaptation — not just physical. It transfers to every movement in every sport.

⚡
Explosive Power

SAQ training develops fast-twitch muscle fibres — responsible for explosive first steps, jumps and rapid changes of direction. Trainable at any age.

🎯
Reaction Time

Agility drills train the brain-to-muscle connection. Athletes respond faster under pressure not because they think faster but because they have trained the patterns.

💪
Injury Resilience

Properly developed movement patterns significantly reduce injury risk. Athletes land better, decelerate safely and have body control to avoid common sports injuries.

📈
Performance Transfer

Athletic foundation training transfers across all sports. A faster, more agile athlete performs better in tennis, football, rugby and every other sport simultaneously.

🌟
Confidence

Athletes who move well carry that confidence into competition. The mental edge from knowing your body can do what is required is as powerful as any technical skill.

The Golden Window

WHY AGE MATTERS
MORE THAN YOU THINK.

Children between 6 and 18 are in a critical period for athletic development — but different windows exist for different qualities.

Ages 6-9: coordination and movement exploration. The nervous system is plastic and patterns formed now last a lifetime.

Ages 10-13: the golden window for speed and agility. Gains made now are retained. Athletes who receive dedicated speed training in this period have a lasting advantage.

Ages 14-18: power and strength can be added. Athletes with the foundation can now build truly elite physical qualities.

Ages 6–9 • Foundation
Coordination Window
Focus: Movement patterns, coordination, balance, fun
Ages 10–13 • Development
⚡ Golden Window
Focus: Speed mechanics, agility patterns, reaction training
Ages 14–18 • Performance
Power Window
Focus: Power, strength, sport-specific performance
Common Myths

WHAT PARENTS
GET WRONG.

✗ Myth
"Speed is natural — you either have it or you don't"
Speed is a skill. Like any skill it can be taught, trained and dramatically improved with the right methods. Genetics set a ceiling — training determines how close you get to it.
✗ Myth
"More hours in their sport will make them faster"
Sport practice improves sport skills. It does not specifically develop the neurological and muscular systems responsible for speed and agility. These require dedicated training.
✗ Myth
"Speed training is just for sprinters"
Every sport requires speed, agility and explosive movement. Tennis, football, rugby, cricket, basketball, hockey — all require the same athletic foundations. All trainable.
✗ Myth
"My child is too young for athletic training"
Age-appropriate speed and agility training is safe, beneficial and recommended from age 6. The earlier the movement foundations are built, the more potential is unlocked.

YOUR CHILD DESERVES
THE EDGE.

For Adults

YOU WERE AN ATHLETE.
YOU STILL ARE.
START MOVING LIKE ONE.

Steady state cardio isn’t enough. The fast-twitch fibres that make you powerful, quick and injury-resilient are disappearing with age — and jogging isn’t stopping it. Sprint training is.

Book Free Consultation → Find Out More
The Science

WHY STEADY STATE CARDIO
ISN’T ENOUGH.

2M+
People Studied
A comprehensive analysis of over 2 million people found that muscle power is a stronger predictor of longevity than strength alone. Not endurance. Not flexibility. Power.
40s
When It Starts
Fast-twitch muscle fibre decline accelerates after your fourth decade. These are the fibres responsible for explosive movement, power output and fall prevention. They don’t respond to jogging.
Type II
The Fibres That Matter
Fast-twitch Type II fibres are your safety net — helping you move quickly, absorb impact and stay injury-free. Research confirms that sprint and power training preserves them. Endurance training alone does not.
Use It
Or Lose It
Long, low-intensity endurance work may actually reduce fast-twitch capability over time. The adults who move like athletes into their 50s and 60s are the ones who never stopped training explosively.
Who This Is For
🏃
Former Athletes
You played sport at a decent level. You want to move like an athlete again, not just stay fit. You miss the feeling of being genuinely fast.
🎾
Active Adults 30+
You train consistently but something is missing. You want power, speed and agility — not just cardiovascular fitness.
💼
Time-Pressed Professionals
You don't have hours to spend in a gym. Sprint and power training is the most time-efficient way to build genuine athletic quality.
🏋️
Gym-Goers Wanting More
You lift, you run, but you've never trained explosively. This is the missing layer that transforms how you move in everything else.
⚡
Masters Competitors
You compete or want to compete at masters level. Structured speed and power work is what separates the field.
🛡️
Injury Prevention Focus
You've had injuries and want to move better, absorb impact more efficiently and build the resilience to stay active for decades.
What We Build
1
Sprint Mechanics
How to actually run fast. Posture, arm drive, acceleration phase, stride efficiency. Most adults have never been taught this — and it changes everything.
2
Explosive Power
Broad jump, bounding, plyometrics. The fast-twitch stimulus your body needs to preserve power output and protect against age-related decline.
3
Agility & Reaction
Change of direction speed, reactive movement, lateral quickness. The movement quality that keeps you sharp in sport and in daily life.
4
Movement Quality
Mobility, stability and correct mechanics under load. Building a body that moves efficiently, absorbs impact well and stays injury free.
5
Benchmarking & Progression
Sprint times, agility scores, power outputs — measured, tracked and improved. You'll know exactly what's getting better and why.
From Ben
"I’m 36 and currently training for masters sprint competition. I know exactly what it feels like to rebuild athletic movement as an adult — the stiffness, the hesitation, the gap between how you remember moving and how you actually move now. I also know how quickly that changes with the right training. This programme exists because I needed it myself."
Ben Cullen — LTA L4 · S&C L4 · SAQ Certified · M35 Sprint Competitor

ELITE ATHLETIC
SQUAD DEVELOPMENT.

Structured speed, agility and athletic development programmes for schools, sports clubs and academies. Proven methods. Measurable results. Delivered by Ben Cullen — LTA L4, S&C L4.

What We Do
01
SCREEN
Identify every athlete's baseline. Speed, agility, movement quality and reaction time — all measured and documented.
02
DEVELOP
Progressive weekly sessions that build real athletic ability. Not just fitness — sport-specific speed and movement skills.
03
MONITOR
Track progress with regular benchmarks. Individual reports and squad data so coaches see exactly what's improving.
04
IMPROVE
Faster athletes. Better movers. Fewer injuries. Results that directly translate to performance on the pitch, court or track.

Jog 2x40m • Hip mobility • Lateral walks • Explosive skips
Pro Agility
6 sets • 45s rest
Sprint 5m right, 10m left, 5m right • record times
505 Cut
4 sets each side
Sprint 5m, plant and cut 180°, sprint back
Reaction Drill
3 sets • 6 reps
Coach signals left or right • athlete sprints immediately
Cool Down
5 mins
Walk 2x40m • Hip flexors • Quads • Hamstrings 45s each

Drill Library — 16 Drills
Foundation → Intermediate → Advanced. Master each level before progressing. Every drill includes a coaching cue — the single most important thing to focus on.
Wall Drives
Acceleration
FOUNDATION
Hands flat on wall at 45°. Drive alternate knees up powerfully. The single most important drill for sprint acceleration.
Coaching Cue
"Drive the knee to 90°. Think piston, not pendulum."
Sets
4
Reps
10 each leg
Rest
30s
Falling Starts
Acceleration
FOUNDATION
Video coming soon
Lean forward progressively until you reach the point of no return, then sprint. Teaches the body to find the correct lean angle instinctively.
Coaching Cue
"Let gravity create the lean. Don't force it — feel the fall."
Sets
6
Reps
20m
Rest
45s
Resisted March
Acceleration
FOUNDATION
Video coming soon
March forward with high knees against a band or partner. Builds hip flexor strength for a powerful drive phase.
Coaching Cue
"Full arm swing, 90 degrees. Arms are as important as legs here."
Sets
3
Reps
20m
Rest
40s
3-Point Start
Acceleration
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
One hand on ground, athletic position. On go, explode into a 10m sprint. Teaches maximal first-step power from a low drive angle.
Coaching Cue
"Stay low for the first 5 steps. Coming upright too early kills acceleration."
Sets
6
Reps
15m
Rest
50s
A-Skip
Speed Mechanics
FOUNDATION
Video coming soon
Exaggerated skipping with high knee drive and active forefoot strike. Primary drill for ingraining correct sprint mechanics at low intensity.
Coaching Cue
"Tall posture, slight forward lean. Strike the ground under your hip, not in front."
Sets
4
Reps
30m
Rest
30s
B-Skip
Speed Mechanics
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
A-skip with an additional leg extension cycle. Develops the full sprint cycle including the crucial pawing motion at ground contact.
Coaching Cue
"The cycle should feel like scraping mud off your shoe as you strike down."
Sets
4
Reps
30m
Rest
35s
High Knees
Speed Mechanics
FOUNDATION
Video coming soon
Running with maximum knee drive at high cadence. Develops stride frequency and neuromuscular coordination.
Coaching Cue
"Arms drive the rhythm. Fast elbows = fast legs. Keep them at 90 degrees."
Sets
4
Reps
20m
Rest
30s
Flying 20s
Max Velocity
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
10m gradual build into 20m maximum effort. Goal is peak velocity — the build eliminates fatigue as a limiter.
Coaching Cue
"Relax at top speed. Tension is the enemy. Loose jaw, loose shoulders, fast feet."
Sets
5
Reps
30m total
Rest
90s
T-Drill
Change of Direction
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
Sprint 10m, shuffle right 5m, left 10m, right 5m, backpedal. Gold standard agility test across all sports.
Coaching Cue
"Stay low in the shuffle. Never cross your feet. Touch each cone with your hand."
Sets
4
Reps
Full drill
Rest
60s
505 Cut
Cutting
ADVANCED
Video coming soon
Sprint 5m, plant outside foot, cut 180°, sprint back. Develops safe and powerful directional change.
Coaching Cue
"Plant wide on the outside edge of the foot. A narrow plant causes ankle injuries."
Sets
4 each
Reps
Full drill
Rest
60s
Pro Agility
Sport Speed
ADVANCED
Video coming soon
Centre, sprint 5m right, 10m left, 5m back. Trains the change of direction pattern used in every team sport.
Coaching Cue
"Accelerate hard out of every cut. The decel-to-accel transition is the actual skill."
Sets
6
Reps
Full drill
Rest
60s
L-Drill
Advanced Agility
ADVANCED
Video coming soon
Sprint 5m, cut 90° left, sprint 5m, cut 90° right, sprint 5m back. Sharp-angle direction change at speed.
Coaching Cue
"Shorten your stride 2 steps before each cut. Cutting at full stride causes slipping."
Sets
4 each
Reps
Full drill
Rest
75s
Broad Jump
Explosive Power
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
Standing two-foot takeoff, maximum horizontal distance, controlled landing. Tests and trains explosive hip extension.
Coaching Cue
"Swing the arms hard. Landing should be soft — absorb it, don't crash into it."
Sets
4
Reps
5 reps
Rest
45s
Bounding
Power Endurance
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
Exaggerated running stride with maximum power through each ground contact. Develops elastic strength and hip extension power.
Coaching Cue
"Think of each bound as a single-leg broad jump. Maximise ground contact power."
Sets
4
Reps
30m
Rest
60s
Depth Drops
Reactive Strength
ADVANCED
Video coming soon
Step off a 30-40cm box, land on both feet and immediately jump maximally. Develops the ability to absorb and redirect force instantly.
Coaching Cue
"Minimal ground contact time. The ground is hot — get off it as fast as possible."
Sets
4
Reps
6 reps
Rest
45s
Single Leg Hops
Stability & Power
INTERMEDIATE
Video coming soon
Consecutive hops on one leg for distance. Develops single-leg power and the stability needed to transfer force during sprinting.
Coaching Cue
"Land on a slightly bent knee. A locked knee on landing is an injury waiting to happen."
Sets
3 each
Reps
10 hops
Rest
40s
Knowledge Hub
Four in-depth articles on the science behind your programme. The more you understand why, the better you train.
Performance Science
HOW YOUR BRAIN LEARNS TO RUN FAST
Speed is a neurological skill before it's a physical one. Understanding how the brain encodes movement patterns explains why training method matters far more than training volume.
The Myelin Factor
Every time you perform a movement correctly, a neural pathway fires and gets wrapped in myelin — a fatty sheath that dramatically increases signal speed. More myelin means faster, more automatic movement. This is why 100 correct reps beats 1,000 sloppy ones. Poor mechanics practiced thousands of times become deeply wired poor mechanics.
Why Fatigue Ruins Speed Training
The central nervous system cannot distinguish between "tired but trying" and "this is my maximum speed." If you sprint when fatigued, you are literally training your nervous system to produce slower movement. Quality and rest between reps matters as much as the reps themselves.
The Role of Sleep
Neural consolidation — the process by which the brain permanently encodes new movement patterns — happens during deep sleep. Athletes sleeping less than 7 hours show measurably slower skill acquisition. The session plants the seed. Sleep grows it.
Athletic Development
THE LONG-TERM ATHLETE: WHY RUSHING DEVELOPMENT COSTS YEARS
The most common mistake in youth sport is optimising for short-term performance at the cost of long-term athletic potential.
What the Research Shows
Athletes who specialise in one sport before age 13 are statistically more likely to plateau in their mid-teens. Early specialisation builds sport-specific patterns at the expense of athletic foundations — the very foundations that become the ceiling for later development.
The Critical Windows
Ages 6-9: movement variety and coordination. Ages 10-13: the golden window for speed mechanics — the nervous system is uniquely plastic and patterns here persist for decades. Ages 14-18: strength, power and sport-specific application. Trying to install foundations at 16 that should have been built at 11 is costly.
What This Means For You
Your programme is designed around this framework. The drills, progressions and intensities are calibrated for where your athlete sits in their development arc — not just their current performance level.
Injury Prevention
THE 3 MOST PREVENTABLE SPRINT INJURIES
Most sprint injuries are predictable. They're almost never bad luck — they're caused by specific mechanical errors or training errors that compound over time.
Hamstring Strain
Occurs during the deceleration phase or at peak velocity. The hamstring is overwhelmed by the eccentric load of slowing the leg. Prevention: Nordic curls twice per week, never sprint cold, gradual velocity build in the first 3-4 reps of every session. This is non-negotiable.
Achilles Tendinopathy
Caused by rapid increases in sprint volume, particularly on hard surfaces. The tendon needs 48-72hrs to recover from maximal loading. Prevention: never two max-intensity sessions back to back. Monitor weekly sprint volume. Include calf raises and eccentric heel drops in your between-session routine.
Hip Flexor Strain
Almost always a sign of overstriding — reaching the foot out in front of the body instead of striking under the hip. Prevention: correct wall drives, daily hip flexor mobility and proper acceleration mechanics. This injury is almost entirely a mechanics problem.
Nutrition & Recovery
FUELLING AN ATHLETE: THE COMPLETE GUIDE
You cannot out-train poor nutrition. For speed athletes specifically, what you eat and when you eat it directly impacts training quality, recovery rate and long-term adaptation.
Pre-Session
Speed training runs on glycogen. Sessions in a depleted state produce lower quality reps, greater injury risk and slower neural adaptation. Eat a carbohydrate-rich meal 2-3 hours before, or a lighter snack 45-60 mins before. Avoid high-fat meals immediately pre-session.
The 30-Minute Window
In the 30 minutes after a sprint session, muscle protein synthesis is elevated and glycogen resynthesis is accelerated. This is the most important nutritional window for an athlete. Aim for 20-40g protein and 40-80g fast carbohydrates. Chocolate milk is one of the most effective and accessible recovery options.
Hydration
A 2% reduction in body water causes measurable performance decline — reaction time slows, power drops. For a 50kg athlete that's just 1 litre of fluid. Start sessions fully hydrated. Thirst is a lagging indicator — by the time you're thirsty, performance is already impaired.
Warm-Up Library
Four warm-up and cool-down protocols. Never skip the warm-up — it's not injury prevention theatre, it's performance preparation.
Pre-Sprint Warm-Up — Standard
12-15 mins
Easy jog 2×40m — nothing hard, just circulation
Walking high knees 20m — hip mobility and activation
Walking quad stretch 20m — pull heel to glute, alternate
Walking hamstring scoops 20m — straight leg reach, alternate
Lateral shuffle 2×15m each direction
Carioca 2×20m — coordination and hip rotation
A-skip 2×20m — begin grooving sprint mechanics
Stride 2×40m at 70% — build into pace gradually
Stride 2×40m at 85% — approaching sprint pace
Pre-Speed Session — CNS Activation
10-12 mins
Jog 2×30m easy
Ankle circles 10 each direction — critical for sprint health
Hip flexor stretch 30 secs each side — kneeling
Leg swings forward/back 10 each leg
Leg swings side to side 10 each leg — glute activation
Explosive skips 2×15m — light, quick ground contacts
Bounding 2×20m at 60%
Falling starts ×3 at 80% — acceleration without full sprint effort
Pre-Agility Session — Movement Prep
10 mins
Jog 2×20m easy
Butt kicks 2×20m — quad and hip flexor activation
High knees 2×20m
Lateral shuffle 2×15m each direction
Forward lunge with rotation 5 each side
Cone touch drill 60 secs at 50% — activate change of direction
T-drill ×2 at 70% — groove the pattern before full effort
Post-Session Cool-Down
8-10 mins
Easy walk/jog 2×40m — begin parasympathetic recovery
Standing quad stretch 45 secs each side
Kneeling hip flexor stretch 45 secs each side
Seated hamstring stretch 45 secs each side
Glute stretch (figure-four) 30 secs each side
Calf stretch 30 secs each side — Achilles protection
10 slow deep breaths — nervous system downregulation
Recommended Reading
Book
The Talent Code
Daniel Coyle
How deep practice and myelin development create exceptional skill. Directly relevant to why your programme is structured the way it is.
Book
Speed Training for Young Athletes
Keith Miniscalco
The definitive practical manual for youth speed development. Technical, evidence-based and age-appropriate.
Book
Endure
Alex Hutchinson
The science of performance limits and what the brain actually controls. Changes how you think about effort and potential.
Book
The Sports Gene
David Epstein
Nature vs nurture in athletic performance. Nuanced and fascinating — challenges assumptions about talent and training.
Book
Bounce
Matthew Syed
The myth of natural talent. Syed's own story as a world-class table tennis player trained through deliberate practice.
Framework
Long-Term Athlete Development
Balyi, Way & Higgs
The evidence-based framework underpinning this programme. Understanding LTAD explains why we do what we do at each age and stage.
For Parents
HOW TO SUPPORT YOUR ATHLETE BETWEEN SESSIONS
Praise the process, not the outcome
Comment on effort and correct mechanics, not speed. "You drove that knee really well" lands better than "you were so fast." Athletes praised for effort are more resilient to setbacks.
Let them rest
Pushing for extra training between sessions undermines the programme. The rest days are where adaptation happens. A tired athlete in the next session learns nothing and risks injury.
Feed them well
Protein within 30 minutes of a session. Carbs before. Water consistently throughout the day. These three things matter more than any supplement.
Watch without coaching
If you watch sessions, stay silent on technique. Two voices with different cues creates confusion. Your job in the stands is to be supportive, not instructive.
Ask good questions
After a session, ask "what did you work on today?" not "how did you do?" The first builds reflection and growth mindset. The second creates anxiety about performance.
Celebrate the small wins
A 0.1 second improvement in the T-drill is huge. These moments matter enormously to young athletes — notice them, name them, celebrate them.

JUMP PROGRAMME
Plyometric power development · Block 1 Foundation required
Prerequisites
This programme requires Block 1 Foundation complete. Plyometric training places high loads on tendons and joints. Athletes need proper landing mechanics and movement quality first. Rushing this causes injuries.
Power
Explosive Force
Height
Jump Higher
Landing
Safe Mechanics
Speed
Fast Twitch
4-Week Jump Block
WEEK 1 — LANDING MECHANICS
Before we jump high we learn to land safely. This week is entirely about absorbing force correctly.
SESSION 135-40 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Jog 2x30m • Ankle circles • Leg swings • Explosive skips
Depth Drop
4 sets • 8 reps
Step off 30cm box • land soft • focus on absorption
Broad Jump
4 sets • 5 reps
Max distance • stick every landing before going again
Single Leg Balance
3 sets • 45s each
Eyes closed last 20 secs • stability foundation
Cool Down
6 mins
Walk • Calf stretch 45s • Quad stretch 45s
SESSION 235-40 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Jog • Dynamic stretches • Ankle bounces • Hip circles
Box Jump Low
4 sets • 6 reps
30cm box only • perfect landing every rep • step down
Lateral Bounds Slow
3 sets • 6 each
Slow and controlled • stick every landing
Single Leg Hop
3 sets each • 4 hops
Short distance • land and hold 2 secs before next hop
Cool Down
6 mins
Easy jog • Hip flexors • Hamstrings
WEEK 2 — LOAD THE SPRING
Landing mechanics are solid. Now we build explosive power through the full plyometric range.
SESSION 135-40 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Full dynamic warm up • plyometric prep
Box Jump
4 sets • 6 reps
35-40cm box • explosive take-off • soft landing
Consecutive Broad Jumps
3 sets • 4 jumps
Max distance • controlled rhythm
Tuck Jump
3 sets • 6 reps
Drive knees to chest • full extension before tuck
Cool Down
6 mins
Full stretch • foam roll calves
SESSION 235-40 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Jog • skips • ankle bounces • explosive prep
Lateral Bounds
4 sets • 8 each
Explosive push • drive opposite knee • stick landing
Single Leg Hop Distance
3 sets each • 5 hops
Max distance each hop • record total
Depth Drop to Jump
3 sets • 6 reps
Drop then immediately jump • introduce reactive element
Cool Down
6 mins
Walk • Hip flexors • Calf stretch
WEEK 3 — PEAK POWER
Maximum effort week. Every rep is full power. This is where the adaptation happens.
SESSION 140 mins
Warm Up
10 mins
Full CNS activation • plyometric prep sequence
Depth Jump
4 sets • 5 reps
Step off box • land and jump immediately • minimal contact
Max Box Jump
4 sets • 5 reps
Maximum height you can land safely • personal best attempt
Consecutive Broad Jumps
4 sets • 5 jumps
Maximum distance • record total each set
Cool Down
8 mins
Full stretch • recovery focus
SESSION 240 mins
Warm Up
10 mins
Full dynamic warm up • top speed activation
Lateral Bound Complex
4 sets
5 lateral bounds then immediate 10m sprint
Tuck Jump Max
3 sets • 8 reps
Maximum knee height every rep • no pacing
Single Leg Hop for Distance
4 sets each • 5 hops
Personal best distance • record both legs
Cool Down
8 mins
Easy jog • full lower body stretch
WEEK 4 — RETEST AND RESULTS
Retest everything. See exactly how much higher you are jumping. Submit scores for Block 2.
SESSION 140 mins
Full Warm Up
10 mins
Complete warm up protocol
RETEST: Broad Jump
4 attempts
Compare to Week 1 • record best
RETEST: Box Jump Height
4 attempts
How high can you jump now?
RETEST: Single Leg Hop
4 hops each
Compare to Week 1 • record total distance
Cool Down + Celebrate
8 mins
Mark the improvements — they matter
SESSION 240 mins
RETEST: Consecutive Broad Jumps
3 sets • 5 jumps
Compare to Week 1 • record best set
RETEST: Tuck Jump Quality
3 sets • 6 reps
Ben scores 1-10 vs Week 1
Free Choice Drill
15 mins
Pick your favourite drill from the block
Score Submission
5 mins
Submit all retests to Ben for Jump Block 2 code

Jump Drill Library — 8 Drills
📦 Box Jump
PLYOMETRIC
Stand in front of a box 30-40cm. Jump with two feet, land softly on top with bent knees. Step down never jump down. The king of plyometric training.
Coaching Cue
"Drive the arms up as you jump. Arms create 30% of jump height. Soft landing — absorb it."
Sets
4
Reps
6 reps
Rest
45s
🔄 Depth Drop
PLYOMETRIC
Step off a box and land on both feet absorbing the impact. Focus entirely on landing mechanics before progressing to depth jumps.
Coaching Cue
"Land quiet. Loud landing means you are crashing into the floor, not absorbing force."
Sets
4
Reps
8 reps
Rest
30s
⚡ Depth Jump
PLYOMETRIC
Step off box, land and immediately jump maximally. Minimum ground contact. Only for athletes who have mastered the depth drop.
Coaching Cue
"The ground is hot. Get off it as fast as possible. No pause between landing and takeoff."
Sets
4
Reps
5 reps
Rest
60s
🦘 Broad Jump
PLYOMETRIC
Two-foot takeoff, maximum horizontal distance, controlled landing. Tests and trains explosive hip extension.
Coaching Cue
"Swing the arms back then forward aggressively. Hip extension and arm drive together."
Sets
4
Reps
5 reps
Rest
45s
🏃 Consecutive Broad Jumps
PLYOMETRIC
5 broad jumps in a row without stopping. Develops power endurance and repeated explosive efforts.
Coaching Cue
"Each jump maximum effort. If you slow down, rest longer next time."
Sets
3
Reps
5 jumps
Rest
60s
1️⃣ Single Leg Hop
PLYOMETRIC
Consecutive hops on one leg for maximum distance. Develops single-leg power and landing stability critical for sport.
Coaching Cue
"Land on a bent knee. A straight knee on landing is an injury waiting to happen."
Sets
3 each
Reps
5 hops
Rest
45s
🔀 Lateral Bounds
PLYOMETRIC
Explosive lateral jump from one leg to the other. Develops horizontal power and single-leg strength used in cutting and direction change.
Coaching Cue
"Push off the outside edge of the foot. Drive the opposite knee up. Stick each landing."
Sets
4
Reps
8 each
Rest
40s
🪜 Tuck Jumps
PLYOMETRIC
Jump vertically and pull knees to chest. Develops explosive power and hip flexor strength for maximum jump height.
Coaching Cue
"Drive the knees up not the jump down. Full extension before you tuck."
Sets
3
Reps
8 reps
Rest
45s
A Note From Ben
"Plyometric training is the most powerful and the most easily misused form of athletic development. Done correctly and at the right time it produces dramatic improvements in jump height, speed and power. Done too early it causes injuries. That is why this programme only unlocks after Block 1 — the foundations have to come first. Trust the process."
Ben Cullen — LTA L4 · S&C L4 · SAQ Certified
🔒
FOUNDATIONS FIRST
The Jump Programme requires solid athletic foundations before you start. Athletes who skip this step get injured. Complete Block 1 of your main programme first — then unlock this with the code Ben sends you.
Incorrect code. Complete Block 1 and message Ben.

JUMP PROGRAMME
Plyometrics & Power Development — Foundations Required
Landing
Mechanics
Explosive
Power
Reactive
Strength
Jump
Height
Before You Start
Jumping higher is not just about leg power — it is about learning to produce force rapidly and absorb it safely. Athletes who skip the landing mechanics phase get knee and ankle injuries. Every session starts with a landing quality check before any explosive work.
Week 1 Sessions
SESSION 130-35 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Jog 2x30m • Leg swings • Hip circles • Ankle bounces • Bodyweight squats x10
Depth Drop — Landing Focus
3 sets • 8 reps
20cm box only • Focus entirely on landing quality • Soft and controlled
Squat Jump
4 sets • 8 reps
Full squat depth • Maximum explosion • Arms drive overhead
Broad Jump
4 sets • 5 reps
Two-foot takeoff • Maximum distance • Record best
Cool Down
6 mins
Walk 2x30m • Quad stretch 45s • Calf stretch 45s • Hip flexor 45s each
SESSION 230-35 mins
Warm Up
8 mins
Jog 2x30m • Dynamic leg swings • Skipping • Light ankle hops 2x10
Box Jump
4 sets • 5 reps
Start low (30cm) • Land softly • Step down, don’t jump down
Single Leg Hops
3 sets each leg • 8 hops
Controlled consecutive hops • Stick the landing on final hop
Tuck Jump
3 sets • 8 reps
Continuous • Drive knees high • Maintain rhythm
Cool Down
6 mins
Full lower body stretch sequence • Foam roll calves if available
Jump Drill Library
8 progressive drills from landing mechanics to elite reactive strength. Work through them in order.
Squat Jump
Power Foundation

Stand feet shoulder-width, squat to 90° and explode upward as high as possible. Arms drive up on takeoff. The fundamental plyometric building block.
Coaching Cue
"Drive through the whole foot. Think push the floor away, not jump up."
Sets
4
Reps
8 reps
Rest
45s

Standing two-foot takeoff, maximum horizontal distance, controlled landing. Develops the explosive hip extension that underpins all jumping ability.
Coaching Cue
"Swing the arms hard. Soft landing — absorb through ankles, knees, hips."
Sets
4
Reps
5 reps
Rest
45s

4
Reps
5 reps
Rest
50s

3
Reps
8 reps
Rest
30s

Step off box, land and immediately jump maximally. Develops the stretch-shortening cycle — absorbing and redirecting force instantly. Elite plyometric.

A Note From Ben
"Jumping higher is the most visible athletic skill there is. Every sport rewards it — basketball, volleyball, football headers, gymnastics, athletics. But the athletes who jump highest aren’t the ones who trained jumps first. They’re the ones who built the foundations first and then trained jumps. That’s why this programme has a prerequisite. Do it right."
— Ben Cullen · LTA L4 · S&C L4

SESSION 145-50 mins
Full CNS Warm Up
10 mins
Jog 3x40m • Dynamic leg swings • Carioca • A-skip • B-skip • Explosive skips • Falling starts x3 at 80%
Split Step Complex
4 sets • 8 reps
Fed ball from service line. Split step on toss • explode to called side • simulate shot • recover to centre
Resisted Sprint to Court Sprint
4 sets
10m resisted • release • 15m court sprint • decelerate and split step. Max CNS output.
505 Cut with Racket
4 sets each side
Sprint 5m, plant outside foot, cut 180° • simulate swing through the cut
Repeat Sprint Test — Baseline
6 reps • 20m
Full effort with 20s rest. Record every rep. Week 1 fatigue index baseline.
Cool Down
8 mins
Easy jog • Hip flexors 60s • Hamstrings 60s • Achilles 45s each
SESSION 245-50 mins
Reactive Warm Up
10 mins
Jog • Reaction ball 2 mins • Mirror drill 2x30s • Lateral band walks • Explosive side steps

4 sets • 5 reps
40cm box • land and immediately sprint 10m to cone • split step • recover
Pro Agility Timed — Baseline
6 reps • full rest
Record every time. Week 1 baseline. Target sub 4.4s.
Lateral Bound to Split Step
4 sets • 6 reps
3 lateral bounds • land in split step • explode to called side
Footwork Ladder Complex
3 sets
High knees • lateral shuffle • in-in-out-out • zig-zag at max speed
CNS Cool Down
8 mins
Walk 3 mins • Full lower body stretch • Deep breathing protocol

Performance Knowledge
Pro Performance Science
THE MARGINAL GAINS THAT SEPARATE TOP 500 FROM TOP 1000
At ATP level the technical and tactical gaps between players ranked 500 and 1000 are smaller than most people think. The real differentiators are athletic. First step speed. Recovery speed between shots. Ability to maintain movement quality in the 5th set. These are physical qualities — and they respond to training. The athletes who close these gaps do so through structured, progressive athletic development. Not more hitting. Not more conditioning runs. Specific neuromuscular work targeting exactly the movement patterns that tennis demands.
CNS Management
WHY RECOVERY IS AS IMPORTANT AS TRAINING
At professional level the central nervous system is the limiting factor — not fitness, not strength. The CNS controls every explosive movement: first step, split step, direction change. Overload it without adequate recovery and performance drops dramatically. This programme is designed around CNS load management. Hard sessions have minimum 48 hours recovery. No max intensity speed work the day before a match. Sleep is non-negotiable — 8+ hours is when neural consolidation happens.
Court Movement
THE SPLIT STEP IS YOUR MOST IMPORTANT ATHLETIC SKILL
The split step is not a technique — it is an athletic skill. Timed correctly it pre-loads the muscles with elastic energy at the exact moment the opponent strikes the ball. This converts their energy into your first step. A 50ms improvement in split step timing has been measured to increase court coverage by 60cm per rally. Over a 3-set match that compounds into a completely different level of court access. This programme trains split step timing systematically — not as a tennis skill, but as an athletic reflex.
Speed Science
TOP SPEED VS FIRST STEP — WHAT ACTUALLY MATTERS IN TENNIS
Tennis is almost never played at top speed. The average rally movement is 3-5 metres. What determines court access is the first 2-3 steps — the phase where elastic strength, CNS reactivity and first step mechanics determine whether you arrive in balance or scrambling. This is why this programme prioritises first step explosion, reactive strength and split step mechanics over conditioning runs. Fitter does not mean faster in tennis. More explosive does.
Week 1 Baseline Benchmarks
Record all baseline scores in Week 1. These are your starting point. Every 4 weeks we retest and measure the delta. Progress is the metric — not where you start.
20m Sprint
Secs — flying start

From Ben
"At professional level the technical gaps are small. The athletic gaps are where matches are won and lost. First step speed, split step precision, the ability to move at 80% pace for 3 hours — these are trainable. This programme targets exactly those qualities. Let's measure where you are, build the programme around what you need most, and track every improvement. The marginal gains are real — and they compound."
— Ben Cullen · LTA L4 · S&C L4 · SAQ Certified

Tennis Movement Knowledge
Court Science
THE SPLIT STEP — WHY IT CHANGES EVERYTHING
The split step is the single most important athletic skill in tennis. Done correctly it allows you to pre-load your muscles at the exact moment the opponent hits. A late split step means you're always reacting. An early split step means you're always ready. The timing is everything: land as they strike.
Movement Mechanics
WHY TENNIS PLAYERS GET SLOW
Most junior tennis players move slowly not because they lack speed but because they move incorrectly. Backpedalling to retrieve a lob loses two full steps vs turning side-on. Running through the ball on recovery prevents the split step. These are habits, not limitations — entirely coachable.
Athletic Development
THE COURT COVERAGE EQUATION
Court coverage is not about top speed. It's about first step speed, decision speed and movement efficiency. A player who takes 0.1 seconds longer to read the ball and 0.1 longer in their first step reaches the ball 60cm later. At club level that's attack vs defence. At elite level it's in vs out.
Injury Prevention
PROTECTING YOUNG TENNIS PLAYERS
The three most common junior tennis injuries — ankle sprains, knee pain and hip flexor strains — are almost all movement injuries. They happen when athletes plant incorrectly, backpedal when they should turn, or lack hip mobility. This programme builds the patterns that prevent all three.
A Note From Ben
"You’re already investing in tennis coaching. This zone is the piece that sits underneath it. The split step timing, the first step explosion, the lateral court coverage — these are athletic skills, not tennis skills. Build the engine and the tennis takes care of itself."
— Ben Cullen · LTA L4 · S&C L4
